item.content:encoded:
I think I figured it out?

It's the physical manifestation of the mystery of the Foundation itself. The radios represent a failure of communication, and the book represents the database. The people attempting to describe the book fall silent because it's classified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for exactly the same reason. How can this reach +100 (before I downvoted) anyway? I read the whole comment section and it seems that nobody else gets it either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't give us enough to piece together the mistery you present. It's well presented, I'll give you that. I would compare it with being given a delicious dessert and then having our mouths sewn shut.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My impression started off bored and ended clueless.
Found myself looking for spoiler in the forum section.

But, I don't know, I don't think it's that bad - just not my cup of tea
I do admit 'copy of Foundation site out of nowhere' theme feels a bit old but not old enough to be prohibited from using it - no problem there for me at least
And this relationship between fake personnels and the floating book - certainly got me wondering what's behind and that can't be a bad sign
Just wish that you provided more leads though..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is a bit odd going through it and as many others have stated a bit of a jumble of ideas thrown together but I gave it an upvote once I read the radio transmissions. I think that gave it a little bit more coherency despite throwing it into more shadow than it already had. Its mysterious nature in the end helped win it over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This felt just boring. I don't mind "anomalous (fake) Foundation site" being played played relatively straight and even one or two of the concepts used here could lead to a really interesting, but in the end, not only do you pile up a whole bunch of stuff to oversaturation, but you end up simply never seriously picking up my interest. That is, in my opinion, even worse than building up to something and not delivering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't really grab me. Doesn't inform me enough to be grabbed. Don't see any hints of hidden connections. It's Switzerland vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just question why they would think to put umlauts on the a when they could just as easily have written "Nee-nah Kare-deh". Assuming the personnel in question are native English speakers, anyway. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
several instances of "it is believed that " or "it appears to be " when the "it" in question clearly is what it is

One can never be certain with this sort of anomaly. SCP-2423-2 is an object locked in unbreakable geosynchronous orbit 2 meters above the floor in a facility that spontaneously materialized out of nowhere. No matter how much it may look and feel like a book, its a good idea to maintain a healthy sense of skepticism about these things. For comparison, SCP-2540 appears to be a lime. It is entirely reasonable to suspect that SCP-2540 may not, in fact, be a lime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was gonna upvote because of the whole "logic tantalisingly out of reach" thing, but I eventually arrived at novote because of some parts of the article, namely: several instances of "it is believed that " or "it appears to be " when the "it" in question clearly is what it is; second interview log is a bit over the top, but slightly excusable given that the anomalous personnel instill some sort of sense of unease; the child speaking through the radio sounds like a cartoon villain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I miss things like that all the time. Thanks for pointing it out.

Edit: Also, it's Nee-na Kare-deh. It's based on words for "tongue" and "rock". I believe them to be Mingrelian, but don't quote me on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, it was originally Long Beach, and I changed that to Santa Clara. Suppose I should have gone with Santa Cruz. Would anyone mind if I switched it to Santa Cruz?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And the people who believe it's apocryphal believe that it's apocryphal all the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted hard at Incident 2423-A.

I love these open mystery ones. A story that answers all of its own questions is a dead story; it leaves nothing more to be told, nothing that keeps you thinking about it, nothing that creates more story beyond its own words. The best type of worldbuilding you can have is a story that answers questions with more questions- each of which is potentially a story of its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it! It's weird, but feels like there's some underlying logic just tantalizingly out of reach. This one feels challenging but solvable (and doesn't even ever need be solved to keep working at this level).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really neat. I think there is some room for expansion, but I'm +1ing right now. There are a couple spots where tone felt a little wonky, and things like giving the interviewees random extra-#'s to show that we've catalogued them all would help. Also, how is "Nina Kärde" pronounced? That's a very specific spelling to have picked up from an audio recording.

incidents of SCP-2423-1

I think you mean "instances".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree with this sentiment. When a reader or critic fails to pick up some nuance I had in the document, then I consider that my fault. I'm the author, it's my job to convey meaning to the reader.

In this case (I am not the author here), there is clearly not enough information to piece the mystery together. I can fit the pieces together in multiple internally consistent narratives. The question to me is, does this make the piece poorer, in my opinion? Not for me. I accept the ambiguity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "You can fool all the people some of the time, and some of the people all the time, but you cannot fool all the people all the time." A quote attributed (apocryphally, according to some) to Abraham Lincoln.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But all of us? Call it the Abe Lincoln Principle.

I feel like I'm missing a reference, because this is a cool name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll go +1 just because I think there may be more here than anyone is seeing.

I don't often take exception to another user's criticism of a piece within a discussion thread, but I think this is a general enough issue that it needs to be addressed, here or elsewhere.

If everyone who reads this is failing to pick up on whatever is going on here, then that represents a failure on the part of the writer. If indeed there is anything going on here. This community, for as much as I tend to disagree with it on the whole, is discerning enough to pick up on contextual clues and subtle themes that my baseline assumption is never "wow, the writer must just be too clever for us." I might miss something and not get a piece. You might miss something. But all of us? Call it the Abe Lincoln Principle.

You're free to upvote for whatever reason you like, of course. But the burden of proof, in my mind, is on the writer to effectively communicate the story they are trying to tell. I do not believe that burden to be on the reader to invest meaning in something where it might not even be there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there something tying all this together that I'm missing? What the heck is Nina Kärde anyway?

This is either a "grab-bag" as Roget put it, or a very awesome mystery that fits together in a way that I can't seem to figure out.

I'll go +1 just because I think there may be more here than anyone is seeing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the fourth article depicting a fake foundation site that I can remember, and it's ultimately not doing anything for me and not covering any new ground. Echoing the "conceptual grab-bag" comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd definitely swap out Santa Clara with Santa Cruz if you're looking to maintain real-world verisimilitude. Santa Cruz was the basis for Santa Carla in The Lost Boys, anyway.

The level of mystery remains high in this piece. Why are people angered by the 2423-1? What are the radios talking about? Is 2423 from here, from elsewhere, or spontaneously generated, or other?

However, I still feel like this works. We don't know the how or why for 2423, but we do know what to do about it. I think that's why it's okay to let the mystery remain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like a grab bag of disparate, vaguely radio-themed elements thrown together and half-baked. The stuff like the number of staff being squares of whole numbers, the instances causing irritation or whatever, the weird floating book what can't be moved, all of this feels like a collection of weird-for-the-sake-of-weird ideas mashed into a narrative that's not really understandable on any thematic level. As a result, I feel completely unmoved by this.

I'll also second the sentiment that what you're describing is not Santa Clara.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool, +1. One little quibble: Santa Clara, California is not touristy at all, and not especially near the beach—it's in Silicon Valley. What the interviewee describes sounds more like Santa Cruz.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After thinking more about this article after I first read the draft, I think I've arrived at a novote. While the development is interesting, ultimately, I'm left with just too many questions and a nonspecific threat at the ending for the impact. While it was well-written, that's just not enough to convince me to upvote.
